bytes.topic: Embedded Fundamentals — microcontroller vs microprocessor, bare metal vs RTOS
bytes.title: esp32: Microcontroller vs Microprocessor - Bare Metal vs RTOS
bytes.body: The esp32 contains a dual-core Xtensa LX6 microprocessor. However, when working at the hardware level, you interact with the microcontroller, which includes the CPU, peripherals, and memory. Bare-metal programming means writing code that directly interacts with hardware registers. This is useful for low-level tasks like interrupt handling and peripheral configuration. On the other hand, using a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) like FreeRTOS provides a higher-level abstraction, allowing you to focus on application logic while the RTOS handles tasks scheduling and synchronization.

bytes.topic: Arduino — setup/loop model, libraries, shields, hardware vs software serial
bytes.title: ESP32 — Optimizing Arduino Setup/Loop with Serial Communication
bytes.body: The ESP32's Arduino setup() function runs once at startup, while loop() runs continuously. In serial communication, choose between hardwareSerial and SoftwareSerial libraries. SoftwareSerial is slower and more prone to errors, but useful for multiple serial connections.
bytes.code_snippet: const char* ssid = "your_wifi_ssid"; const char* password = "your_wifi_password"; void setup() { Serial.begin(115200); } void loop() { Serial.println("Hello World"); delay(1000); }

bytes.topic: Real-time Constraints — deadlines, jitter, priority inversion, watchdog timers
bytes.title: esp32: Mastering Real-time Constraints with Deadlines, Jitter, and Watchdog Timers
bytes.body: When implementing real-time systems on esp32, managing deadlines, jitter, and priority inversion is crucial. The esp32's FreeRTOS prioritizes tasks based on their configuration. Use `xTaskCreate()` with the `pxCurrentTCB` argument to assign a fixed priority. To combat jitter, enable the `configUSE_PORT_OPTIMISED_SCHEDULER` flag in `FreeRTOSConfig.h`. This flag optimizes the scheduler for esp32's port. Additionally, use watchdog timers with `esp_timer_create()` to enforce deadlines and detect task timeouts. For example:

bytes.topic: Raspberry Pi — Linux on embedded, GPIO via Python/C, choosing Pi vs microcontroller
bytes.title: esp32 - GPIO via Python vs C: When to Choose Raspberry Pi
bytes.body: When working with the esp32, developers often face a dilemma: whether to use a Raspberry Pi for Linux-based development or stick with microcontroller-specific code. esp32's built-in GPIO capabilities make it ideal for low-level I/O operations. However, complex tasks benefit from the power of Linux on a Raspberry Pi. For instance, consider using a Raspberry Pi for data logging or signal processing, where Linux's processing capabilities can be leveraged. In contrast, esp32's microcontroller nature is better suited for time-sensitive tasks like motor control or sensor readings.

bytes.topic: Power Management — sleep modes, wake sources, battery life optimization
bytes.title: esp32 Power Management: Maximizing Battery Life with Sleep Modes
bytes.body: The esp32's power management system is designed to minimize power consumption. The `esp_sleep()` function is used to enter deep sleep modes, including `ESP_SLEEP_MODE_NONE`, `ESP_SLEEP_MODE_LIGHT`, `ESP_SLEEP_MODE_DEEP`, and `ESP_SLEEP_MODE_ULP`. To wake from deep sleep, use `esp_add_wake_source()` with a `WAKE_SOURCE_TOUCH` or `WAKE_SOURCE_EXT0` for touch or external pin wake, respectively. For example, to wake from deep sleep on touch, set `WAKE_SOURCE_TOUCH` and call `esp_sleep_enable_touch_wakeup()`. To optimize battery life, use `esp_deep_sleep()`, which disables peripherals, and set `ESP_SLEEP_MODE_DEEP` and `WAKE_SOURCE_TOUCH`.

bytes.topic: Peripheral Communication — reading sensors, driving actuators, protocol selection
bytes.title: esp32 - Choosing the Right I2C Protocol for Your Sensor
bytes.body: When communicating with I2C sensors on the esp32, you'll often encounter the choice between standard (100 kHz) and fast (400 kHz) modes. The esp32's I2C peripheral supports both modes, but the decision depends on your sensor's requirements. For example, the popular BME280 temperature and humidity sensor operates at 100 kHz, while the MLX90614 infrared thermometer requires the faster 400 kHz mode. To switch modes, use the `i2c_set_clock_divider` function. Set the divider to 10 for standard mode (100 kHz) or 2 for fast mode (400 kHz). Ensure the sensor's clock speed matches the mode you select to avoid data corruption.

bytes.topic: Choosing a Platform — Arduino vs ESP32 vs Raspberry Pi vs STM32 decision framework
bytes.title: ESP32 Dev: Arduino vs ESP32 vs Raspberry Pi vs STM32 — Decision Framework
bytes.body: When choosing a platform for your ESP32 project, consider the following factors: 1) Complexity: If you're new to ESP32, the Arduino IDE provides an easier learning curve. 2) Resource requirements: ESP32 has a smaller footprint compared to Raspberry Pi. 3) Performance: For demanding tasks, consider STM32. 4) Development time: Use ESP32 if you need rapid prototyping. 5) Libraries and community: ESP32 has extensive libraries and a large community.

bytes.topic: Networking on Embedded — MQTT, CoAP, HTTP on constrained devices
bytes.title: esp32: Choosing the Right Networking Protocol for Constrained Devices
bytes.body: When working with esp32, selecting the right networking protocol for IoT applications is crucial. MQTT, CoAP, and HTTP are popular choices, but each has its trade-offs. MQTT is ideal for low-bandwidth, high-latency networks, while CoAP excels in constrained networks with limited resources. HTTP, on the other hand, is more suitable for applications requiring web-based interfaces. esp32's IDF library provides native support for all three protocols. Use `esp_mqtt_client_init` for MQTT, `coap_client_init` for CoAP, and `http_client_init` for HTTP. For instance, to initialize an MQTT client, use `esp_mqtt_client_init(&client, config, port, timeout)`. When choosing a protocol, consider the device's resources, network conditions, and the application's requirements.
